sketch0.title: Introduction to Text Concatenation in SQL

intro-concat.title: Introduction to Text Concatenation in SQL
intro-concat.bodyMarkdown:
Concatenation means joining two or more text values together to form a single string. In SQL, you can use the `||` operator to concatenate text fields or literal strings. For example, to combine a customer's name and city, you could write:

```sql
SELECT full_name || ', ' || city AS name_and_city
FROM customers;
```

If any field is `NULL`, the result of concatenation will also be `NULL` unless you handle it with functions like `COALESCE`.

concat-null.title: Handling NULLs in Concatenation
concat-null.bodyMarkdown:
When concatenating columns, if one of the columns is `NULL`, the result will be `NULL`. To avoid this, use the `COALESCE` function to provide a default value. For example:

```sql
SELECT full_name || ' (' || COALESCE(city, 'Unknown') || ')' AS display_name
FROM customers;
```

This ensures that even if `city` is `NULL`, the output will show 'Unknown' instead.

concat-literals.title: Concatenating with Literal Strings
concat-literals.bodyMarkdown:
You can concatenate columns with literal strings to format your output. For example, to create a label with the customer's name and status:

```sql
SELECT full_name || ' - Status: ' || status AS label
FROM customers;
```

This produces results like 'Mia Lopez - Status: active'.

single-choice-concat-operator.title: Which operator is used for concatenating text in SQLite?
single-choice-concat-operator.prompt: Select the correct operator for concatenating two text columns in SQLite.
single-choice-concat-operator.hint: It's the correct answer double symbol, not the correct answer function.

help.concept: In SQLite, the `||` operator is used to join two or more text values together.
help.hint_1: It's not the plus sign or the correct answer function call.
help.hint_2: Look for the operator that looks like two vertical bars.

multi-choice-concat-output.title: Concatenation Output Examples
multi-choice-concat-output.prompt: Given the row: full_name = 'Emma Reed', city = 'Miami', which of the following SQL expressions will output 'Emma Reed, Miami'?
multi-choice-concat-output.hint: Remember the correct operator and order.

help.concept: Concatenation in SQLite uses `||` and the order of columns matters for the output.
help.hint_1: The correct answer uses `||` and puts the name before the city.
help.hint_2: Ignore options that use `+` or the CONCAT() function, as those are not valid in SQLite.

drag-reorder-concat.title: Order the Steps: Creating a Custom Label
drag-reorder-concat.prompt: Arrange the steps to create a label like 'Olivia Chen (Chicago)' from the customers table.
drag-reorder-concat.hint: Think about the order of text in the final label.

help.concept: Concatenation involves selecting columns and literal strings in the desired order, then joining them with the concatenation operator.
help.hint_1: Start with the name, then add the opening parenthesis, then the city, then the closing parenthesis.
help.hint_2: The operator is used to join all the selected parts together.

tokens.t5: Use the || operator to join all parts

fill-blank-choice-coalesce.title: Using COALESCE to Handle NULLs
fill-blank-choice-coalesce.prompt: Fill in the blank to ensure that if city is NULL, 'Unknown' is shown instead: SELECT full_name || ' (' || ______(city, 'Unknown') || ')' FROM customers;
fill-blank-choice-coalesce.hint: This function returns the first non-NULL value.

help.concept: The the missing term function returns the first non-NULL value from its arguments.
help.hint_1: It's a standard SQL function for handling NULLs.
help.hint_2: It starts with 'C' and is used for fallback values.

code-input-concat-email-label.title: Create an Email Label
code-input-concat-email-label.prompt: Write a SQL query to select all customers and show a new column called 'email_label' that combines full_name and email in the format: 'Mia Lopez <mia@example.com>'. If email is NULL, show 'No Email' instead.
code-input-concat-email-label.hint: Use concatenation and COALESCE for the email part.

help.concept: Combine columns and literal strings using the `||` operator, and use COALESCE to handle NULL values.
help.hint_1: Place the email inside angle brackets and use COALESCE for missing emails.
help.hint_2: The pattern is: name, space, '<', email or default, '>'.
